item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image within this article is non-compliant as the original source is here. As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m writing an article about another orangutan and I wish to reference this SCP. Make it it’s mother, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before reading the full thing, I imagined this is about an orangutan constantly in pain from being on fire and setting everything she loves or cares about on fire (like her home forest), before the foundation rescues her and contains her in a pool.

I guess I got most of it right. I think pain from being on fire could make this interesting though.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
This skip is short, simple, and sweet. Not every skip has to be a meta-encyclopedia (even if I do love me some meta-encyclopedias.)

Anyways, here's wonderall: https://youtu.be/s-oNjzHKli4

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A concise and interesting article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 'cause I was thinking just the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2984 is a female Bornean orangutan (Pongo pygmaeus) entirely consumed in fire.

Surely if it's entirely consumed in fire, there's nothing left?

I'm on no-vote/upvote territory with this one. Sheesh I just don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.
It's one of many examples where the 'clinical tone' of an SCP report gives an interesting contrast with the contents of the report: it's a flaming orangutan, so what? Take it in stride!

I feel sorry for this poor girl on fire (Must confess a special tenderness for orangutans) - and at the same time, being contained and taken care of by the Foundation is probably the best that can happen to her. So, all in all, this article gives a very pleasant feeling of 'all is as right as can be with the world of the Foundation'.

Upvoted.
<Eta Carinae strolls away, dreaming of hugging SCP-2984>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is right square in the middle of neutral vote country. It's a technically proficient writeup of a clearly anomalous phenomenon. The writeup clearly is well considered and displays effort on the part of the writer. It doesn't have any unnecessary elements, and I can imagine it being an article that the casual site reader would happen upon during a Series III binge and enjoy.

It's also sort of a by-the-numbers article of a kind that we have plenty of here, and I don't feel particularly moved by it. Part of that is how jaded one gets after reading thousands of articles, though. I can't get behind it, but I won't advocate for its removal either.

I have no idea whether the title annoys me or amuses me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Subject became agitated and began to roam aimlessly through the village, leaving behind embers and soot (later determined to be fecal droppings and hair follicles)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So does a flaming orangutang fling flaming poop?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what if it has Sheer Force

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That picture totes looks like it's taking a selfie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Get it halfway to death and see whether it turns into a statue.

EDIT: http://i.imgur.com/nPs7WWS.png

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is this Darmanitan?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an orangutan which is on fire, which is pretty great, considering…

But it's just an orangutan that's on fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is on the extreme end of conceptual simplicity. It has only one real draw in its favour, and that's the central image. Thankfully, it's a good central image (the burning ape) and the image doesn't outstay its welcome. The 'cuteness' or whatever of this article doesn't really attract me in any way, but this is the closest things I can see on the site to the sort of thing you hear about when researching cryptozoology or UFO sightings, and I can upvote that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very well-written, cute, and has a lot of good thought exploring the realities of the anomaly. I really want to upvote it.

But the anomaly… it's Johnny Storm. It's a Johnny Storm orangutan.

In the past I've downvoted or no-voted articles for having a thorough and nuanced exploration of a boring idea. Those weren't quite as good as this… but still, I'll need to think about this one and come back to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much what I had to say. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My usual standard for pronouns: "Subject" for living things, "Object" for not living things

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That sounds like your opinion, man. A good SCP is like a joke, and SCPs that explain everything with excessive backstory are the SCP equivalent of explaining the joke within the joke itself.

It is, indeed. I may beg to differ on what a good SCP entails. Anyway, that does not change the fact that this is a good thing to have a mental image over.

But seriously, why use the term "subject"? Any specific reason over other options? Or just for the heck of it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it lacks in a narrative behind it. There are honestly a lot of questions one can ask when they see such a thing. Are there more of it? Why is it like that?

+ show block
– hide block

This resembles older SCPs, whose weight is carried by Tales that mention then than the elaborations of the article itself.

That sounds like your opinion, man. A good SCP is like a joke, and SCPs that explain everything with excessive backstory are the SCP equivalent of explaining the joke within the joke itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Subject will return containers to delivery system when finished.

For the sake of clarification, 2984 knows to return food containers. Is it trained to do so?

Pros:

  • Interesting imagery, especially from the recovery log.

Cons:

  • While it tells and shows us 2984, it lacks in a narrative behind it. There are honestly a lot of questions one can ask when they see such a thing. Are there more of it? Why is it like that? This resembles older SCPs, whose weight is carried by Tales that mention then than the elaborations of the article itself.

Verdict: Neutral vote

Minor matter: Why call it "subject" instead of "SCP-2984"?

P.S. Love the nickname for it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is also a description of a flaming orangutan.

Yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One should call an orangutan a monkey at one's own peril. Remember the words of the Librarian: "Ook ook."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article makes me very happy on multiple levels. It is a wonderful exploration of the adaption of living creatures to new circumstances. It is also a description of a flaming orangutan.

+upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a bit anticlimatic in the sense that the backstory doesn't particularly provide anything grander than "rescued", but it's still a sweet little article about a flaming monkey. I wonder how well it'd play with artificial monkeys, if those were non-flammable.
